data.title: Sterling claws higher after US/UK trade deal
data.description:
Key points:
  • Sterling rises as optimism grows over trade
  • Euro set for biggest four-week loss versus pound since 2022
  • Analysts say US/UK trade deal lacks detail, but supports sentiment
  • BoE cut rates on Thursday, policymakers split on voting

The pound rose on Friday, following the announcement of a "breakthrough" trade deal between Britain and the United States the previous day.

U.S. President Donald Trump and British Prime Minister Keir Starmer on Thursday announced a limited bilateral trade agreement that leaves in place Trump's 10% tariffs on British exports, modestly expands agricultural access for both countries and lowers prohibitive U.S. duties on British car exports.

The deal, while light on substance and unlikely to serve as a blueprint for other countries, analysts said, was enough to offer investors some hope that trade agreements with the Trump administration were possible.

Britain will hold talks on trade with the European Union later this month and the U.S. deal has helped shore up sentiment around that, ING strategist Francesco Pesole said.

"The deal had already been largely priced in, and the implications for the UK economy are not significant," he said. "That said, the UK has now signed two trade deals in quick succession, and that is keeping markets hopeful on trade talks with the EU," Pesole said, referring to UK's trade agreements with the United States and India.

Sterling was last up 0.3% against the dollar at $1.3278, having touched its lowest since mid-April earlier in the day.

Meanwhile, the Bank of England on Thursday cut interest rates by a quarter point to 4.25%, as expected. Voting by policymakers was split three ways, with two members of the rate-setting committee opting for no cut at all, which traders took as a sign to buy sterling, at least initially.

BoE Governor Andrew Bailey on Friday said the trade deal was a good thing but still left tariffs on most British exports to the U.S. higher than they were before last month.

"It's good news in a world where it will leave the effective tariff rate higher than it was before all of this started," he said in a question and answer session at an economics conference in Reykjavik.

The derivatives market shows traders have ditched their bets on another cut from the BoE in June and still only see a little more than a 50% chance of a cut in July.

Sterling was set for a flat week against the dollar, which has benefited from a nascent sense of optimism that more trade deals may be in the offing, with particular focus on this weekend's scheduled talks between U.S. and Chinese negotiators in Switzerland.

Against the euro , however, the pound is on a roll. The euro is heading for its largest four-week loss against sterling since October 2022, when the pound burst above record lows struck during the crisis triggered by former Prime Minister Liz Truss' failed budget plans.

The euro has lost over 2% against sterling in the last four weeks, with a 1% drop in this week alone.

data.title: Polypropylene (PP) prices remain steady in Europe
data.description: This week, PP prices were left unchanged in the European region.An industry source in Europe informed a Polymerupdate team member, "The European polypropylene market experienced stable prices due to persistently low demand. Activity in the market was minimal, influenced by a public holiday in several countries that resulted in a shortened work week. Although there was a decline in the upstream propylene MCP settlement, spot prices for homopolymer injection and copolymer grades remained unchanged, as weak demand was offset by a tightening supply situation. The European monthly contract for the essential PP feedstock propylene decreased by Eur65/mt compared to April, attributed to a drop in naphtha prices. While buyers anticipated that the reduction in the propylene MCP settlement would be reflected in PP pricing, producers raised concerns regarding the sustainability of their profit margins. Despite the lack of demand, spot prices were supported this week by temporary supply constraints following power outages in Spain and Portugal in April. Contract negotiations are expected to commence soon, following the settlement of the May monomer contracts. However, widespread public holidays across the continent have postponed the initiation of these discussions, which are set to begin next week.Offers for May-delivery PP volumes have not yet been presented in many instances, but a significant disparity is developing between sellers' pricing expectations. Some PP producers are keen to enhance their margins by retaining part of the decrease in feedstock propylene's May MCP, citing low stock levels that alleviate the urgency to sell. Producers aim to capitalize on buyers' need to replenish their stocks during the peak demand season in spring, especially since many buyers had previously restrained their procurement due to the backwardated pricing trend. Additionally, recent production challenges at domestic PP plants have prompted producers to exploit their tighter domestic supply. Nevertheless, the overall sentiment remains pessimistic, with many believing that producers may continue to face difficulties in expanding their margins for the rest of the second quarter.Recently, numerous polypropylene (PP) facilities in Spain and Portugal experienced sudden shutdowns due to an electricity outage affecting the Iberian Peninsula. The restart of these units is occurring in a staggered manner; however, the full restoration of refineries, crackers, and polymer units, along with the normalization of production, may extend beyond a week, assuming no significant damage has occurred. Currently, information is limited, but an initial increase in off-grade PP supply is anticipated. The closure of downstream converters' facilities, coupled with holiday breaks, has alleviated any immediate supply disruptions, although the true impact is expected to become more apparent as purchasing interest resumes in the upcoming weeks. The restoration of converters' operations is relatively less complex compared to that of crackers and polymer units. In other developments, maintenance work has commenced at a cracker in France, while another cracker in northern France remains offline due to a separate power outage last week, which has resulted in technical difficulties. There are indications that this facility may remain offline for several weeks, which will affect the associated polyethylene (PE) and PP units, with reports of production disruptions at the latter. Additionally, maintenance is currently underway at a PP plant in central eastern Europe, and further maintenance is anticipated to begin at a PP facility in the Netherlands at the end of May, with some market participants suggesting this may have been postponed from an earlier scheduled maintenance in April.In the spot markets, PP injection moulding grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1075-1085/mt FD North West Europe mark, while PP block copolymer grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1155-1165/mt FD Northwest Europe levels, both steady from last week.Meanwhile in the contract markets, PP injection moulding grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1465-1470/mt FD NWE Germany and FD NWE France levels, both stable week on week. PP injection moulding grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1455-1460/mt FD NWE Italy levels, steady from last week. Meanwhile, PP injection moulding grade prices were assessed at the GBP 1245-1250/mt FD NWE UK levels, unchanged from the previous week.In the contract markets, PP block copolymer grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1535-1540/mt FD NWE Germany and FD NWE France levels, both flat week on week. PP block copolymer grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1525-1530/mt FD NWE Italy levels, rolled over from the previous week. Meanwhile, PP block copolymer grade prices were assessed at the GBP 1305-1310/mt FD NWE UK levels, constant from last week.The source added, "These developments are expected to result in a diverse and fragmented supply landscape in May, where the underlying inventories and production forecasts of producers will play a crucial role in shaping their pricing strategies. Buyers, on the other hand, will advocate for a reduction in the May MCP for feedstock propylene. They will argue that the significant disparity with spot prices is essential for a comprehensive adjustment of their May contract prices. Conversely, producers are likely to contend that they must expand their profit margins, citing the unfavourable conditions in the petrochemical sector that continue to jeopardize the financial viability of polypropylene (PP) plant operations. Nevertheless, producers may gain some advantage from decreased import volumes from the Middle East and Asia-Pacific, attributed to challenging economics associated with long-haul imports and limited arbitrage opportunities against domestic prices, which have deterred risk-taking initiatives. Additionally, producers in the Middle East and Asia may shift their focus towards the Chinese market, as tariffs on propane imports from the United States impact the economics of Chinese propane dehydrogenation (PDH) plants, potentially leading to shutdowns of some of these facilities. This situation could trigger a chain reaction of shutdowns at certain PP plants in China due to a tighter and more costly supply of feedstock propylene. However, there have been indications of progress in ongoing discussions regarding a possible exemption for feedstock imports from the US, highlighted by the recent announcement that ethane will be exempt. Should the trade negotiations advance and yield an exemption for US-origin propane imports to China, any relief from reduced PP imports to Europe may only be temporary for domestic producers, as sellers in other regions could then redirect their focus to the European market. In addition to the immediate need for restocking, the market conditions in Europe are anticipated to remain difficult, resulting in sustained low demand. This situation is primarily due to a largely stagnant economic forecast, with the possibility of a recession still not entirely dismissed at this point. Apart from certain applications that gain from the dynamics of fast-moving consumer goods, it is expected that buyers will limit their purchases to essential needs and largely avoid speculative risks. Polypropylene (PP) is particularly vulnerable to this trend, given its ties to consumer durables and the rigid packaging sector, as well as high-value purchases and the automotive industry. Demand for these items is projected to stay low due to diminished consumer confidence and a decrease in purchasing power. Although it is premature to make definitive statements, any significant rise in upstream crude oil production and the resulting impact on feedstock prices could indicate a potential backwardation in PP pricing. Should this scenario occur, it may prompt buyers to further reduce their stock levels, aiming to replenish their inventories at lower prices in the upcoming months.FCA Antwerp PP homopolymer prices were assessed at the Euro 1055-1085/mt level while FCA Antwerp PP copolymer prices were assessed at the Euro 1135-1165/mt levels, both left unchanged week on week.Upstream propylene spot prices on Thursday were assessed at the Euro 845-855/mt FD Northwest Europe levels, a marginal rise of Euro (+5/mt) from last week.European Propylene contract price for May 2025 settled at the Euro 1015/MT FD North West Europe levels. This price represents a sharp decline of Euro 65/mt from its April 2025 settlement levels.

data.title: Trade hopes feed risk appetite, boosting stocks and spurring bitcoin
data.description:
Key points:
  • U.S. deal with UK is first since Trump's tariff pause
  • U.S. officials to meet with Chinese negotiators on Saturday
  • European stocks open higher
  • Bitcoin leaps to highest since January; gold falls for third day
  • Chip shares rise in Japan, Taiwan; China stocks struggle

World stocks hovered around their highest prices in six weeks after a U.S. trade deal with Britain fueled guarded optimism for progress in tariff talks with other countries.

MSCI's broadest index of world shares EURONEXT:IACWI gained 0.1% after jumping around 0.8% on Thursday, to levels seen just before Trump's 'Liberation Day' global tariff announcements.

"The deal between the U.S. and UK was more style over substance," said Kyle Rodda, a senior financial markets analyst at Capital.com.

The "general terms" agreement leaves in place a 10% tariff on goods imported from the UK but lowers prohibitive U.S. duties on UK car exports. Britain agreed to lower its tariffs to 1.8% from 5.1% and provide greater access to U.S. goods.

"However, it feeds the narrative that the U.S. is looking to bang out rapid-fire trade deals and reduce tariffs - at the margins - and other trade barriers," Rodda said.

Last week, Trump said he has "potential" trade deals with India, South Korea and Japan.

Trump pushed back against seeing the UK deal as a template for other negotiations, perhaps, including those due Saturday when U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and chief trade negotiator Jamieson Greer will meet China's economic tsar He Lifeng in Switzerland.

European stock markets opened higher on Friday. The pan-European STOXX 600 index rose 0.4%, as of 0915 GMT, with all regional bourses trading higher.

An investor rush from safe assets such as government bonds into riskier ones such as stocks might mean markets are getting ahead of themselves on optimism, said James Rossiter, head of global macro strategy at TD Securities.

"The trade deal isn't really a trade deal. It's an agreement on a few narrow topics. Still, it shows there is a degree of movement and that some tariffs could be mitigated," said Rossiter. Even so, he added, "tariffs are not going away."

Reaction to the UK trade agreement yesterday and the optimistic trade figures that emerged yesterday from China have pushed markets higher temporarily, but "the fundamentals behind what markets are seeing are not as robust," said Rossiter.

Safe haven German Bund prices fell on Friday, driving yields 5.2 basis points higher, as investors dropped their bonds for assets with higher returns.

Bitcoin soared to the highest since January and U.S. crude ticked up after a more than 3% surge on Thursday.

Brent crude added 85 cents to $63.70 per barrel, following Thursday's 2.8% rally. NYMEX U.S. crude skipped up 84 cents to $60.76 per barrel on Friday, building on the previous day's surge.

Standard Chartered's Geoffrey Kendrick no longer sees risk sentiment as the main driver for bitcoin.

"It is now all about flows, and flows are coming in many forms," said Kendrick, the bank's global head of digital assets research, pointing to an influx of cash into bitcoin ETFs, as well as buying by so-called whales.

Shares of crypto exchange Coinbase Global rose over 5% on Thursday after it announced $2.9 bln deal to buy crypto option exchange Deribit. Shares fell on Friday morning 1.1% after a lower first quarter profit report.

The U.S. dollar index , which measures the currency against six major peers, edged away yesterday's one-month peak down 0.3%.

The euro rose from its one-month trough at $1.1257, and sterling ticked up 0.2% to $1.3270.

Mainland China blue chips closed down 0.2%, while Hong Kong's Hang Seng ended 0.4% higher. Japan's Nikkei soared 1.6% and Taiwan's equity benchmark TWSE:TAIEX advanced 1.8%, with technology shares the strongest performing sector.

data.title: Dollar On Track to End the Week Higher
data.description:
The dollar index edged lower to around 100 on Friday, after two straight sessions of gains, as traders looked ahead to US-China trade talks scheduled for the weekend.

President Trump expressed optimism, saying he expects the negotiations to yield tangible progress.

Meanwhile, the newly announced US-UK trade deal provided some relief to investors hoping for a de-escalation in global trade tensions.

However, the deal appears limited in scope, with few details released so far, and a 10% tariff on imports from the UK remains in place.

For the week, the greenback is on track to rise about 0.6%, supported by the Federal Reserve’s decision to leave the fed funds rate unchanged, while signaling no urgency to cut rates and highlighting increased risks to both inflation and unemployment.

data.title: Arabica Coffee Futures Below $4
data.description:
Arabica coffee futures hovered around $3.95 per pound, consolidating below two-month highs of nearly $4.15 hit on April 28, as the ongoing harvest in Brazil takes center stage amid tight stock levels.

State-owned Conab has revised Brazil’s 2025 coffee harvest forecast, now expecting a 2.7% increase to 55.7 million 60 kg bags, up from an earlier estimate of a 4.4% decline to 51.8 million bags.

This new projection is also 2.7% higher than the 2024 crop and marks an all-time high for an "off-year" in the biennial arabica coffee cycle.

data.title: Bund yields rise to multi-week highs on trade hopes
data.description:
Key points:
  • UK-US trade deal spurs optimism for tariff de-escalation
  • US-Chinese officials to hold talks this weekend
  • Euro zone growth environment worsening - Finnish central banker

German Bund prices fell on Friday, pushing yields higher, as investors moved away from safe-haven assets after a U.S.-UK trade deal raised hopes for similar tariff agreements elsewhere, boosting risk appetite.

Markets expect trade tensions to hurt economic growth in the euro area, potentially forcing the European Central Bank to cut interest rates further.

Stocks surged, fuelled by guarded optimism over tariff talks, while futures on the S&P 500 index were up 0.1%.

Germany's 10-year yield (DE10YT=RR), the euro area benchmark, rose 6.5 basis points to 2.588%, its highest since April 11. It's currently on track for its biggest weekly rise, up 6.5 bps, since early March when Germany announced a political agreement to massively increase fiscal spending.

U.S. Treasury yields edged higher in London trade - with the 10-year up 0.5 bps at 4.38% - after touching multi-week highs on Thursday.

U.S. President Donald Trump and British Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced on Thursday a limited bilateral agreement.

Trump said he expected substantive negotiations with China this weekend and predicted that U.S. tariffs on Beijing of 145% would come down.

"Markets have somewhat eased economic fears following President Trump’s signals of a trade war de-escalation. However, this relief may be premature," said Johan Javeus, senior economist at SEB group.

"We expect U.S. data to increasingly reflect the impact of higher import prices and for trade uncertainty to weigh on business investment and hiring decisions."

Money markets priced in an ECB deposit facility rate of 1.65% for December. They had indicated a depo rate below 1.55% in mid-April as the ECB suggested it was ready to cut rates in response to the potential adverse economic impact of U.S. tariffs. The depo rate is currently 2.25%.

The euro zone's fight to bring inflation back to the 2% target is on track but the growth environment is deteriorating, with a global trade war creating a significant risk in the outlook, Finnish central bank chief Olli Rehn said on Friday.

Barclays recently argued that 1.5% terminal rate pricing should be the floor for now, with the ceiling around 1.75%.

"The yo-yo between these levels or beyond these levels would be a function of how the news flow around tariffs evolve and if the hard data eventually matches the weakness in the soft data," Barclays said in a research note.

German 2-year yields (DE2YT=RR), more sensitive to ECB policy rates, were up 3 bps at 1.80%.

Italy's 10-year yield rose 7.5 bps to 3.66% , leaving the spread over Germany's Bund yield - a market gauge of the risk premium investors demand to hold Italian debt - at 105 bps. (DE10IT10=RR)

data.title: Alcoa Appoints Thomas Gorman as Non-Executive Chairman
data.description:
Alcoa said late Thursday it has named Thomas Gorman as non-executive chairman of the board.

Gorman, a board member since May 2021, succeeded Steven Williams, who stepped down after more than eight years of service.

data.title: India's Nifty, Sensex set to snap winning streak as India-Pakistan hostilities rise
data.description:
** India's Nify 50 and Sensex set to snap its longest weekly winning streak of 2025 on heightened volatility as India-Pakistan conflicts intensify

** The indexes down around 1.5% for the week, after rising nearly 7% in the previous three weeks

** India and Pakistan accused each other of launching new military attacks on Friday, using drones and artillery for the third day in the worst fighting between the nuclear-armed South Asian neighbours in nearly three decades

** Sun Pharmaceutical Industries and Jio Financial Services fall nearly 5% each, set to be biggest pct losers among Nifty 50 stocks for the week

** Tata Motors and Titan Company jump 7.7% and 4.8%, on course for biggest weekly Nifty 50 pct gainers

data.title: Indian generic drugmakers set for weekly drop on potential US pharma policy changes
data.description:
** Shares of Indian generic drugmakers down between 2% to 5% this week

** Sun Pharmaceuticals down 4.8% this week, set for sharpest weekly drop since June 2022

** U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order on Monday that aims to reduce the time it takes to approve pharmaceutical plants in the country

** Cipla and Dr Reddy's Laboratories down 3.49% and ~2% this week, respectively

** The three drugmakers, having a combined 46% weightage on the pharma index , get between 30% to 40% of revenue from North America

** Pharma index is down 2.7% this week
